note: Player-facing profile for every tradition: what the craft IS, what it can do at first rank, what mastery looks like, and what it costs. Written for two surfaces — the character-creation guide (pick a tradition knowing what you are choosing) and the Library. Function coverage is derived from the shipped ability content, not authored separately, so it cannot drift. Authored by Aevi (PO) during the SNG-147c per-rank pass, 2026-07-18.

traditions.name: The Harmonic
traditions.oneLine: Sound as structure. You hear what a thing is made of, and you can sing it steadier, quieter, or apart.
traditions.theCraft: Harmonics treat vibration as the honest layer of the world. Everything has a note — a wall, a crowd, a wound, a lie — and a practitioner who finds it can reinforce it, mask it, or break it at the point where it was always going to fail.
traditions.atCreation: You perceive through sound where others need light: bodies through a wall, a digger at work below, the gait of someone you know. You can push objects at a distance, blanket a corner in quiet, or run on a rhythm that carries you further than your legs should.
traditions.withMastery: A chord set once and left ringing that holds a company through a night's march. A room brought to genuine willingness by a voice. A shatterpoint called on a Radiant lattice, a fortress wall, or a body — and struck where it was already going to fail.
traditions.theTrade: Nothing a Harmonic does is quiet. The craft is audible by definition; there is no stealth in it. It works on structure, so it is superb against the made and the massed and awkward against the singular and the strange.
traditions.whoPractices: Choir-trained, mostly. Harmonic and Radiant are closed to each other — a life spent learning to hear is not a life spent learning to see.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Radiant reveals by exposure; Harmonic reveals by resonance. The Radiant makes a thing visible. The Harmonic makes it audible, and then makes it fall.

traditions.name: The Radiant
traditions.oneLine: Light as tool, weapon, and witness. Nothing you shine on gets to stay hidden, and at full focus the beam does more than show.
traditions.theCraft: Radiants work light the way a smith works metal: stored, shaped, thrown, and sharpened. The tradition's premise is that exposure is a good in itself — that what survives being seen clearly is what deserved to.
traditions.atCreation: You read the hidden spectra — heat on a handle, tampering under paint, the flush of a lie in good light. You bend light to hide in, bank daylight in a jar against the night, and coax a season's warmth into a struggling seedbed.
traditions.withMastery: A lattice fence warding a compound for a season. Surgery done by your own operating glow, deep enough to rebuild a crushed hand. And the Sunlance — a single coherent strike through a stone wall, an engine block, or a person.
traditions.theTrade: You glow. There is no Radiant stealth, no Radiant ambush, no Radiant anonymity — the craft announces its user in the act of using it. It fails on mirror-finish, on running water, and in true dark where there is nothing to work with.
traditions.whoPractices: Glasshome-trained, and the blaze-adjacent settlements. Closed to Harmonic, and the two traditions have opinions about each other.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Where the Harmonic finds a thing's note, the Radiant simply shows it. Both end arguments; only one of them does it politely.

traditions.name: Precursor Work
traditions.home: the Old Roads and the lattice under everything
traditions.oneLine: The machinery the world was built on still answers, if you learn its grammar. Never offered at creation — earned in fiction.
traditions.theCraft: Precursor work is not a tradition so much as a conversation with what the builders left running. It is queried, not commanded; it answers on its own terms; and every exchange pulls the practitioner's own coordinate further toward the pole they are drawing from.
traditions.atCreation: Not available. Access opens per-ability through play — a remnant found, a road mastered, a teacher who owes you. Substrate-born origins are seeded a single innate base and grow it like any skill.
traditions.withMastery: Reading a thing's unrepeatable cosmic address and navigating to it across any distance. Foreclosing a possibility outright — and Hold the Aperture, the standing refusal that keeps open what the lattice is closing.
traditions.theTrade: Drift. Every use moves your vector, and Foreclose moves it toward the contraction the framework names as the enemy. The engine tracks it. The GM will eventually name where it has taken you.
traditions.whoPractices: Nobody by right except the substrate-born, and even they only hold the doorway. Everyone else earns each ability separately.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Its living counterpart is the green current; its tangled counterpart is the wild. Same depth, different substrate, and they do not mix.

traditions.name: The Green Current
traditions.home: the Quickwood and every rooted place
traditions.oneLine: The life-layer the Rootkin never surrendered. The ground is awake, and it will answer a true touch.
traditions.theCraft: Where the Precursors built a substrate, the Rootkin kept the one that grew. The green current runs through everything living and connects every rooted place to every other. It answers sincerity and refuses force.
traditions.atCreation: Not available. Innate to rootkin origins, seeded per-ability; earned by everyone else through the Reach.
traditions.withMastery: Waking a whole district's current so that ground you have quickened carries a party rested. Travelling the green road between rooted places, carrying others, toward places you have never walked because something living there knows your touch.
traditions.theTrade: It reaches only where life runs unbroken. A dead zone, a machine-city, a burned waste has no green road through it and no current to wake. And what you wake, you tend.
traditions.whoPractices: Rootkin by birth; Ashwardens and travellers of the Reach by long apprenticeship.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The precursor lattice is fabricated and obeys grammar. The green current is grown and obeys relationship. Neither can do the other's work.

traditions.name: The Churn
traditions.oneLine: Both substrates at once, tangled and generous and never twice the same. It gives — just never exactly what you asked for.
traditions.theCraft: The fae art of productive disorder at the substrate layer. The Churn accretes and improvises. Its gifts are real, potent, and shaped by the current rather than the asker.
traditions.atCreation: Not available. Innate to churnfolk and abyssal origins; opened per-ability.
traditions.withMastery: Naming the shape of what you want and letting the Churn invent every detail. A district-wide flowering of growth-and-ruin that lasts days before the tangle takes it back. Bargains struck with places rather than persons, set to outlive both parties.
traditions.theTrade: No precision, ever, at any rank. You cannot aim it at pure growth or pure ruin. And the Honest Bargain is scrupulously fair, which is the horror rather than the safety.
traditions.whoPractices: The Wild Half — churnfolk, fae, and the Abyssals who kept their hollow.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: It is the third substrate. Precursor work is grammar and green current is relationship; the Churn is neither, and both.

traditions.name: The Somatic
traditions.home: the Flesh-Temples and the Somatic Reaches
traditions.oneLine: The body as a mastered instrument. Not strength — control, past where control should end.
traditions.theCraft: Somatics train the physical self until it answers the will completely: breath, heartbeat, pain, fear, and the precise economy of motion.
traditions.atCreation: Endurance and poise past the ordinary. Control of breath and heartbeat to steady fear, pain, or cold. Motion precise enough to read as grace.
traditions.withMastery: Surviving what kills others. Crossing terrain that stops everyone else. And the whole mastered frame behind a single strike — stance, breath, and timing arriving together.
traditions.theTrade: It is control, not raw force, and it is not thought-work. A Somatic who leans on Second Wind too long is borrowing at a rate the Flesh-Temples consider usurious.
traditions.whoPractices: Flesh-Temple trained. The Middle Way braids it with Noesis; the pure poles cannot.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Noesis is the same discipline pointed at the mind. The Whole Act is what happens when someone refuses to choose.

traditions.name: The Cogitant
traditions.oneLine: Thought sharpened until it acts on the world. Sharp, not wise — the Cogitarium is clear about the difference.
traditions.theCraft: Memory, focus, and reason trained to instrument-grade. A Cogitant models systems and people completely enough to predict them, and holds structure under conditions that break other minds.
traditions.atCreation: Perfect recall of what you attend to. Focus that ignores pain and distraction. Reasoning faster and further than the unpracticed.
traditions.withMastery: A life you can walk back through. Function under cold, injury, grief, and weeks of pressure until the work is done. And thought turned on a person: a conclusion delivered exactly where their reasoning rests, which staggers a mind the way a blow staggers a body.
traditions.theTrade: Sharpness is not wisdom, and this craft least of all. It needs a mind that reasons — the disciplined, the genuinely mad, and the simply uninterested all shrug it off. What you take apart in someone this way, you are responsible for putting back.
traditions.whoPractices: Cogitarium-trained, and the self-taught who found the discipline alone and usually paid more for it.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Soma answers the will through the body; Noesis through the mind. Both are the same refusal to accept an ordinary ceiling.

traditions.name: Wildcraft
traditions.home: the Churn and the unspooling places
traditions.oneLine: Improvisation as power. You are best exactly where the plan has stopped working.
traditions.theCraft: The Churnfolk discipline of productive disorder — riding luck, reading the grain of a collapsing situation, and denying an opponent the predictability they were counting on.
traditions.atCreation: Turn a chaotic moment to advantage. Improvise a working solution from whatever is to hand. Make one plan — yours or theirs — come apart usefully.
traditions.withMastery: Unmaking order at scale: a fortress's discipline, a system's certainty, a plan a season in the making, all coming apart at once — and out of the loosed chaos, the one opening that could not otherwise have existed.
traditions.theTrade: Nothing repeatable. It will not work the same way twice, it offers no fine control, and it cannot give you a result you specified in advance.
traditions.whoPractices: Churnfolk, and anyone whose life has taught them that plans are a kind of superstition.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Latticework is its exact opposite and its best opponent. Each is strongest where the other is blind.

traditions.name: Latticework
traditions.oneLine: Structure as power. What you bind stays bound; what you predict, you have already half-defeated.
traditions.theCraft: Order-craft: plans that hold under pressure, systems read to their load-bearing point, possibilities bound down until they are reliably true.
traditions.atCreation: Build a plan that holds. Predict a system's next move. Impose enough order on a mess to act cleanly inside it.
traditions.withMastery: Structure imposed at scale — a lawless place made orderly, a chaotic force predicted and countered, a possibility foreclosed by sheer structure.
traditions.theTrade: It cannot improvise and it cannot handle the genuinely novel. Anything that must breathe or grow resists it, and Wildcraft is built specifically to make it brittle.
traditions.whoPractices: The Lattice-Cities, their courts, and their engineers.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Wildcraft rides collapse; Latticework prevents it. Riding Order is the braid — unmake a structure and re-bind the falling pieces in the same motion.

traditions.name: The Mason
traditions.oneLine: The real is what you can touch, weigh, and set. Everything else is a claim awaiting proof.
traditions.theCraft: Thingcraft: material read by hand, structures set so they stay, and a flat refusal to grant abstraction any purchase on the real.
traditions.atCreation: Know a thing's true weight, material, soundness, and worth by touch — no lie of appearance survives your hand. Find the gap that is in every wall.
traditions.withMastery: The keystone blow, and where there is no keystone, one manufactured. Whole structures unmade back to raw material. And in your presence, illusion and symbol-working simply fail: the thing is what it is.
traditions.theTrade: The Masons' great blindness is that some things genuinely are not reducible to a touchable fact. The tradition is not good at admitting this.
traditions.whoPractices: The Given Land's builders, and skeptics everywhere.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Formcraft holds that matter is the coarse residue of pattern. The Mason's Denial defeats it outright, which is exactly why Figurists find Masons so tiresome.

traditions.name: The Figurist
traditions.oneLine: Pattern is the finer layer of the real. Draw the figure truly and the world briefly consents to match it.
traditions.theCraft: Formcraft: seeing what a thing is an instance OF, then making symbols that act — marks that do in the world what they mean.
traditions.atCreation: See the pattern behind the fact. Read a set of particulars and name the form they instance.
traditions.withMastery: A standing figure that holds a room to its pattern as long as the mark is undefaced. A figure sent walking with an errand and enough form to run it. And the true name — a handle on a thing's very being, which is also a handle it has on you.
traditions.theTrade: It needs the touchable to be secondary, so a Mason present ruins everything. A false or sloppy figure binds nothing and warns the thing you tried.
traditions.whoPractices: The Pattern Reach, the Lattice courts, and the Cathedral's more dangerous scholars.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Mason and Figurist are the same argument conducted for centuries. Neither has won and both are correct.

traditions.name: Umbracraft
traditions.oneLine: Sight without light, and sanctuary for what the light would destroy. Not evil — hidden, which is a different thing.
traditions.theCraft: The dark-pole mastery: concealment past a knowing searcher, true sight in lightlessness, shadow worked as a material, and the harboring of what should not be exposed.
traditions.atCreation: See clearly where there is nothing to see by. Wrap yourself and a small space in concealment. Put out a lamp at distance.
traditions.withMastery: Shadow commanded at scale — a caravan, a rite, a rebellion hidden. Sanctuary the Blaze cannot breach. And the shadow road: league upon league between distant darks, arriving where no light has been.
traditions.theTrade: Unshadow undoes it, and a Blazeborn at full radiance undoes it everywhere at once. The craft protects and hides; it does not win a direct exchange with someone who can simply make the room bright.
traditions.whoPractices: The Umbral Depths, the sheltered, and everyone who has needed to not be found.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The umbral and the blazeborn are exact mirrors — each has an ability that makes their extreme into food, a healing that works only in their medium, and a road that runs on their element. They are the same person raised in opposite places.

traditions.name: Radiance
traditions.oneLine: Revelation as a moral position, and a beam that ends what should not continue.
traditions.theCraft: The light-pole mastery: exposure that strips every concealment at once, light worked as material, and the full blaze — which is where the tradition stops being about seeing.
traditions.atCreation: Throw light that shows things as they are — no illusion, no disguise, no shadow holds. A focused beam that can burn or blind.
traditions.withMastery: A whole deception undone at once, a hidden thing dragged into the light before a people, and a beam that ends the unendurable. Declarations made in full blaze that those who witnessed cannot pretend they did not see.
traditions.theTrade: You are always visible, and revelation does not discriminate — it exposes allies, and it exposes you. A truth burned free cannot be put back.
traditions.whoPractices: The Radiant Wastes, where intense light is home rather than hazard.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Umbracraft harbors; Radiance exposes. The Blazeborn believe what survives being seen is what deserved to. The Umbral know exactly who that argument has historically been used against.

traditions.name: The Descent
traditions.home: The Descent, and the Abyssal Choir
traditions.oneLine: Appetite named out loud. The Abyssals bargain fairly — that is the horror, not the safety.
traditions.theCraft: The demonic-pole mastery refuses to pretend that want is not the engine. A practitioner of the Descent names what a person actually wants, states the true price of it, and strikes bargains where both sides get what they wanted and both sides pay what it cost.
traditions.atCreation: Feel what someone actually wants beneath what they are asking for. Name a thing's true cost to the person who wants it — they may still take it.
traditions.withMastery: Descending into a person, a place, or an institution and surfacing with its true engine, however it dresses. A price that BINDS once named and accepted. And calling a form up from the deep that holds, acts, and is yours to direct — not docile, but yours.
traditions.theTrade: It catches only what already wanted the bargain; a body with nothing owed offers nothing to hook. Every covenant runs both directions. And The Consumed Wound does not heal — the abyss eats what you give it, and what it ate does not come back either.
traditions.whoPractices: The Abyssal Choir, and everyone who has decided that honesty about hunger beats piety about it.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The Ascent weighs what a person has done; the Descent names what they want. Both see through you completely. Only one of them offers you a deal.

traditions.name: The Ascent
traditions.home: The Ascent, and the Seraphic Orders
traditions.oneLine: Mercy and judgment from the same mouth. The Orders are loved and feared for exactly the same reason.
traditions.theCraft: The angelic-pole mastery sees moral weight directly — what a person has done, what it cost others, what they are still carrying — and can either lift it or pronounce on it. Both are real. Neither is gentle.
traditions.atCreation: See roughly what someone carries: the harm done, the harm borne. Ward one person from one harm with a spoken certainty.
traditions.withMastery: A genuine absolution — a weight lifted, and they feel it leave. A judgment that binds because everyone present knows it is true. And a ward that keeps standing after you have gone.
traditions.theTrade: Weighing anyone at any distance without their knowledge is a power the Orders have never satisfactorily justified. Judgment that binds cannot be taken back, and absolution given wrongly is still given.
traditions.whoPractices: The Seraphic Orders, continuous with the pre-Transition world in a way almost nothing else is.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The Descent's Appetite Strike hits what a person has been protecting by not naming it. The Ascent's Measuring Eye simply reads what they did. Neither pole allows anyone to remain unexamined.

traditions.name: Ruinwork
traditions.oneLine: Ending things well is a craft. Not violence — demolition, with an ethic.
traditions.theCraft: The Unmakers hold that things end whether or not anyone is skilled about it, and that the skill matters. Ruinwork finds where a thing WANTS to break, takes it apart exactly there, and leaves what comes after unpoisoned by how it went.
traditions.atCreation: See where a structure, plan, or relationship is under stress and where it will give. Find the one piece whose failure brings the rest down.
traditions.withMastery: Ending a building, an institution, a life's work, or a war so that what it was is honored. Unmaking a thing meant to be permanent. And returning something complex to the state before it became that specific thing, so the way is clear.
traditions.theTrade: It cannot touch the living or the truly singular, and it does not un-break. Some things were sealed for reasons you learn only after. And un-done is still gone — cleanness is not the same as no loss.
traditions.whoPractices: The Unmade, and every demolitionist, liquidator, and person who has had to end something properly.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The Wright raises; the Unmaker ends. They are the same eye for structure pointed opposite ways, and each is the other's best consultant.

traditions.name: Makecraft
traditions.oneLine: Raise things faster and better than anyone should be able to — and sometimes leave them deliberately unfinished, so they keep going.
traditions.theCraft: The creation-pole mastery: making at speed and at scale, out of whatever is to hand, improved in the making rather than merely replicated.
traditions.atCreation: Make something real and functional, quickly — a bridge, a shelter, a tool, a wall — from what is here, and it will hold long enough to matter.
traditions.withMastery: Work that outlasts you and gets used by people who never learn your name. A masterwork that gathers a lifetime into one teaching thing. And the strange gift at the top of Makecraft: a thing left deliberately incomplete, which KEEPS MAKING ITSELF.
traditions.theTrade: Making truly costs time and care, and cannot be hurried without ceasing to be true. A masterwork can be made once. And a thing built to outlast you will — with everything that carries.
traditions.whoPractices: The Making, the Wrights, and the Enginewrights who took it to world-scale.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Both poles read structure to its load-bearing truth. The Wright uses that to raise; the Unmaker to end. Riding the line between them is its own discipline.

traditions.name: Pathos
traditions.oneLine: Feeling as force. Not manipulation — you make what is actually there undeniable, including when it is yours.
traditions.theCraft: The emotional-pole tradition works with what a room genuinely feels rather than what it performs. A Pathos adept halves a grief by sharing it, carries someone through what they cannot bear alone, and can move a crowd by the simple truth of what everyone in it already feels.
traditions.atCreation: Feel the true emotional state of a person or a room beneath the display. Share a feeling so it eases — a grief halved, a fear steadied.
traditions.withMastery: Feeling at the scale of peoples: a mob turned, a mourning-crowd held, a heart reached that no reason could get to. Laments that let a whole settlement finish a grief together, and names kept so the lost are attended rather than absent.
traditions.theTrade: You carry what you move, for as long as it stays moved. The Threnodists' work lands the whole of a grief on the singer to give it. And the Grief-Strike is the same gift pointed at someone: it opens what they were keeping shut in order to stay functional.
traditions.whoPractices: The Feeling Coast, the Threnodists, and the mourning-singers who go where a loss is too large to carry alone.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Logos proves; Pathos reaches. Each pole can do something to a person the other cannot, and both of them can take someone apart.

traditions.name: Logos
traditions.oneLine: Argument as an instrument. What follows, follows — and at the top of the craft that is a thing you can do to someone.
traditions.theCraft: The logical-pole tradition builds arguments that compel the reasonable, exposes contradictions that cannot be unseen, and constructs systems that run on the soundness of their own reasoning without anyone enforcing them.
traditions.atCreation: Build an argument that holds. Expose a contradiction so it cannot be unseen. Deduce a hidden fact from what is present.
traditions.withMastery: A proof no honest mind can deny. A conclusion a whole body is walked into together, binding against later private doubt. Institutions that run at scale without you because the reasoning inside them is correct.
traditions.theTrade: It proves only what is true — there is no proof of a falsehood, only a forgery the next sound mind unpicks. It fails entirely against anyone who will simply refuse a true conclusion. And to be irrefutably right is a lonely country.
traditions.whoPractices: The Reasoned Hold, the Syllogists, and the courts and schools that grew up around them.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: A Syllogist and a Threnodist looking at the same grieving person see two true things and disagree about which one to act on.

traditions.name: Falsecraft
traditions.oneLine: A fiction built well enough to function. The Veilwrights' first skill is spotting one, because they know the joinery.
traditions.theCraft: Falsecraft builds untruths that hold under scrutiny — disguises, false histories, illusions that persist unwatched — and unbuilds other people's. A practitioner sees construction, which is why the tradition's best liars are also its best detectors.
traditions.atCreation: Craft a convincing illusion or disguise. Tell a lie built well enough to hold. And spot a constructed untruth, because you know how one is made.
traditions.withMastery: A deception a whole people believes. A veil set and abandoned that is now a feature of the place. A false reality someone OPERATES inside, making correct decisions against a picture you supplied. And the Useful Lie — a fiction so complete it is functionally true while it goes unexamined.
traditions.theTrade: It holds only while unexamined; a necessary fiction meets a determined truth and loses, always, eventually. Verity undoes it directly. And the gravest risk is to the maker: tell a lie well enough and you may forget it was one.
traditions.whoPractices: The Veiled Reach, and everyone who has needed a story that lets people keep going.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The Better Story sits exactly on the line — a reframing that heals and is ALSO true. Both poles claim it, and neither is wrong.

traditions.name: Verity
traditions.home: The Reach of the plain word
traditions.oneLine: See what is so, say it plainly, and let it land. The lonely craft — being right is not the same as being welcome.
traditions.theCraft: Verity sees through illusion and the ordinary lie, strips deception bare in front of others, and speaks truths that cannot be quietly taken back. At its height it establishes facts in the record of the real.
traditions.atCreation: See through illusion, disguise, and the ordinary lie. Know when a spoken thing is false. Speak a plain truth others cannot easily deny.
traditions.withMastery: A deception undone before a whole people. A buried thing dragged into day. A word spoken so truly it cannot be unsaid — and the Whole Truth, which cracks even a lie built entirely from true pieces.
traditions.theTrade: It cannot spare what it reveals, and it does not choose whose secret surfaces. You learn things you cannot unlearn, and the powerful will kill to keep some truths buried. Revealing is not compelling — you can be completely right and change nothing.
traditions.whoPractices: Verists, investigators, and the Cathedral of Convincing's opponents — who need them precisely because the Cathedral lies with true pieces.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Falsecraft asks what a person can carry; Verity asks what is so. The Veilwrights think that is cruelty and the Verists think the alternative is contempt.

traditions.name: Enginecraft
traditions.oneLine: Mechanism as a language you speak. The Precursor doors that still half-listen, listen to you.
traditions.theCraft: Enginecraft reads a mechanism's design and purpose on sight, builds reliable machines, and commands machine-systems to reconfigure toward an intent. Alone among the traditions it can run Precursor systems that Latticespeak could only knock on.
traditions.atCreation: Understand a device or machine-system's function and flaws at a glance. Fix a broken tool or machine reliably. Make a device do something within its mechanism it was never set to do.
traditions.withMastery: Machine-country commanded — a whole Precursor installation bent toward a purpose, a mechanical site woken or stilled at scale. And the Deep Works: a mechanism or edifice at the scale the dwarven-mechanical peoples are named for, which changes the maker in the building of it.
traditions.theTrade: It needs a mechanism to talk to; the grown, the wild, and the genuinely novel offer it no purchase. A Deep Work demands a season of yourself and is permanent — the world now has this thing, whatever it turns out to mean.
traditions.whoPractices: The Gearlands, the Enginewrights, and the Deep Works' dwarven-mechanical peoples.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Enginecraft asks what a thing DOES; Numenwork asks what it MEANS. What Sleeps Under is the deep peak of this axis, and neither pole is comfortable about it.

traditions.name: Numenwork
traditions.oneLine: Attention as a force. Attend to something completely enough and the material softens around what it means.
traditions.theCraft: Numenwork perceives the meaning-layer directly — where the world is thin, where something is pressing back — and where significance is dense enough, makes it briefly literal. Its highest form is attention that HOLDS: keeping open, by significance alone, something that was closing.
traditions.atCreation: Feel the significance-density of a place or moment and know where the world is thin. Steady a person in crisis through presence alone.
traditions.withMastery: A thin place opened in a room that had no reason to be sacred. A symbol made briefly real, a threshold held open long enough for others to pass. And a foreclosing thing kept open by nothing but sustained attention.
traditions.theTrade: Nearly all of it needs meaning already present — Numenwork concentrates significance, it rarely manufactures it from nothing. And the Dimmed Meaning is the same craft inverted: drain what made a thing matter and those who relied on it lose their orientation entirely.
traditions.whoPractices: The Numinous Reach, and those who have attended to one thing long enough that it started attending back.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: The Enginewright repairs what is broken; the Numen holds open what is closing. Both are refusals to let a thing end badly.

traditions.name: Spanwork
traditions.oneLine: Distance is a claim, not a fact. Argue with it long enough and it gives.
traditions.theCraft: The space-pole tradition treats ground as negotiable — covering it far past what a body should, closing a distance you can see without crossing the between, and finding the crossing the land insists is not there.
traditions.atCreation: Never be lost: your bearing, your distance, and the shape of the way ahead. Cover ground far past what a body should — a day's march in an hour, tirelessly.
traditions.withMastery: Distance stops being an obstacle for you and those you carry. A crossing made where the land calls itself uncrossable. And the Edge of the Map — the step past the known world entirely, where arriving is what calls the new place into being.
traditions.theTrade: Beyond the map the destination is unfixed until you make it, so you cannot know what you are stepping into, and there is no quick way back. Horizon-walkers are famously bad at staying.
traditions.whoPractices: The Horizon Reach, its scouts, its couriers, and everyone who left.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Both poles refuse a limit everyone else accepts. The Horizon-walker refuses distance; the Hourkeeper refuses duration.

traditions.name: Hourcraft
traditions.oneLine: Duration is workable. Hold a moment open, stretch it, spend it — or bend how long something takes.
traditions.theCraft: The time-pole tradition perceives a moment whole while it is still happening, slows duration to make room inside it, and at its height remembers forward — an hour that has not happened arriving with a memory's specificity and a memory's unreliability.
traditions.atCreation: Know exactly how long since a thing happened and how long until a thing due will come. Hold a moment exactly — perceive it whole, at length, while it is still occurring.
traditions.withMastery: An hour held open past its passing for what must be finished inside it. One possible future traced to its very end. And the Shaped Duration, which bends how long any running process takes — including the ones running inside a person.
traditions.theTrade: Foreseeing binds: what you have seen changes how you act, and acting changes the future you saw. And the Hourkeepers are honest that the Shaped Duration is the ugliest thing they teach, precisely because it never has to touch anyone.
traditions.whoPractices: The Kept Hours, its archivists, and the ones who sit with the dying because they can tell how long.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Space says the far place is a place you may be. Time says the moment is longer than it looks. Both are arguments with a limit, and both eventually win.

traditions.name: The Marcher
traditions.home: The Riven Marches and the Redline
traditions.oneLine: Force done correctly, under forms, with the whole cost visible. Not brutality — competence about the worst thing.
traditions.theCraft: The violence-pole tradition holds that force will be used whether or not anyone is skilled about it, and that skill is what separates a fight that ends from a fight that spreads. Marchers read a fight before it takes shape, control ground, disarm rather than kill where the forms allow, and keep people alive inside it.
traditions.atCreation: See a fight's shape and a foe's tell a beat early. Disarm or disable a single opponent without killing them. Improvise a usable weapon from whatever is at hand.
traditions.withMastery: A whole field controlled, opponents standing where they did not choose to stand. A group kept fighting past what they should survive. And Last Form — a formal duel ended in one undeniable exchange, because you read them completely and answered perfectly.
traditions.theTrade: The forms are real constraints: Last Form fails against a foe who refuses the duel or has no form to read, and killing where the forms forbid costs a Marcher their standing. Competence at violence does not make you right about when to use it.
traditions.whoPractices: The Riven Marches, the Redline's dueling culture, and soldiers who lived long enough to get good.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Stillcraft ends fights by making them unnecessary; the Marcher ends them by being better at them. Both are trying to make the violence stop.

traditions.name: Stillcraft
traditions.oneLine: Peace as an active craft, not an absence. Truces that hold, ground that stays neutral, and a hard edge of its own.
traditions.theCraft: The peace-pole tradition halts conflicts before they break, reads what each side actually needs underneath what they demand, and binds truces to something both sides genuinely value so the peace outlasts the mood that made it.
traditions.atCreation: De-escalate a tense moment before it turns. Read the true feeling in a gathering and find who is about to break the peace. Stand between two parties at the moment before harm.
traditions.withMastery: Peace at the scale of peoples — a generational feud ended, a war stood down. Sanctuary woven into a place deeply enough to survive sustained pressure. And a peace bound so well that breaking it costs more than the war would, with both sides able to see that.
traditions.theTrade: Stillcraft is slow and it needs both sides to have something they actually want. And it is not gentle: The Price Shown puts a reckoning in a person that they carry afterward and cannot unthink — an act of force performed by people who have chosen not to use any other kind.
traditions.whoPractices: The Stillhold, its brokers, and the ones who walk into rooms nobody else will enter.
traditions.acrossTheAxis: Both poles are honest that violence is coming. They disagree about whether the answer is to be excellent at it or to make it unnecessary — and the Marches and the Stillhold need each other more than either admits.
